1: Life cycle assessment of contemporary Canadian egg production systems during the transition from conventional cage to alternative housing systems: Update and analysis of trends and conditions
Ian Turner a, , Davoud Heidari , Nathan Pelletier a
a University of British Columbia, 3333 University Way, Kelowna, BC, Canada

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Life cycle assessment Egg Laying hen Canada Alternative hen housing|The supply-managed Canadian egg industry produces over 789 million dozen eggs per year, the majority of which are produced in conventional cages (~60%). Recently, the industry committed to a complete transition to alternative (i.e. enriched cage, single- and multi-tier free run, free range, and organic) production systems by 2036. This transition may have significant sustainability implications. Here, we present updated (i.e. based on 2019 data) LCA models and results representing the cradle to farm gate environmental impacts of Canadian egg production systems based on a much expanded data set compared to previous models. Generally, input and emission levels decreased across all housing systems, with few exceptions, compared to previously reported levels. Acidifying and GHG emissions decreased across most housing systems due to in- creases in feed- and pullet-use efficiency, while eutrophying emissions increased across all housing systems modeled due to differences in manure management systems. Feed inputs represented the greatest contributor to most impact categories (~18% - 84%), followed by pullet production and manure management (~ 10% - 37% and ~0.01% 62%, respectively). Organic production systems had the lowest impacts in nine of the ten cate- gories assessed, while free range systems generally performed the worst. Conventional cages generally had lower impacts than all non-organic systems. However, it can be expected that as farmers gain experience with alter- native systems resource-use efficiency levels will increase and environmental impacts will decrease. Continued monitoring of environmental performance of Canadian egg production systems is therefore imperative to ensure net-positive outcomes during this housing system transition.
GHG|greenhouse gas LCA|life cycle assessment ISO|International Organization for Standardization CGSB|Canadian General Standards Board N|Nitrogen P|Phosphorous
2: Life cycle assessment of contemporary Canadian egg production systems during the transition from conventional cage to alternative housing systems: Update and analysis of trends and conditions

3: On a global scale, food systems make large contributions to many environmental impacts (Pelletier and Tyedmers, 2010), including up to one third of anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Crippa et al., 2021) and similar or larger shares of other environmental impacts

- a disproportionate amount of which is attributable to livestock pro- duction systems (Marques et al., 2020). Although considered relatively low impact, egg production is among the fastest growing industrial livestock sectors, particularly in areas that have experienced significant increases in affluence and individual purchasing power (Preisinger, 2018; UN FAO, 2020). Egg production in Canada has followed a similar trend, with the Canadian egg industry now producing in excess of 789 million dozen eggs per year (Egg Farmers of Canada, 2020a).

While the majority of egg production in Canada (i.e. 62%) takes place in conventional cage housing systems, the Canadian egg industry is currently undergoing a complete transition to alternative (i.e. enriched cage, single- and multi-tier free run, free range, and organic) production systems, scheduled for completion by 2036 (National Farm Animal Care Council, 2017). Similar transitions have already taken place in many jurisdictions globally (see for example Luke et al., 2016;

Matheny and Leahy, 2007; The Council of the European Union, 1999). While primarily driven by the growing status of farm animal welfare as an area of concern for consumers (Clark et al., 2016; Miele and Lever, 2013; Vanhonacker et al., 2012), there are also potentially significant implications for the environmental sustainability of egg production associated with this transition, due to reported differences in environ- mental impacts between systems (Dekker et al., 2011; Leinonen et al., 2012; Pelletier, 2017). These differences, and trade-offs between them, must be identified and quantified to facilitate informed decision making by farmers and policy-makers regarding the sustainability implications of this transition.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is one tool that may be used to investi- gate the environmental impacts of egg production systems. LCA seeks to assess the cumulative resource demands and environmental impacts of a product throughout its entire supply chain. LCA has been applied to many egg production systems globally, including in the Netherlands (Dekker et al., 2011), the United Kingdom (Leinonen et al., 2012), Spain (Abín et al., 2018), and many others. These studies consistently identify feed and pullet inputs, as well as manure management practices, as major contributors to a wide array of environmental impacts. Results from these studies may provide valuable insight to farmers and policy makers by identifying priority targets and potential interventions for improvement of environmental sustainability outcomes of egg produc- tion, such as the assessment of strategies for improving nitrogen-use efficiency (Zargar Ershadi et al., 2021).

Recently, Pelletier (2017) performed the first LCA of the Canadian egg industry, differentiated by housing system type, using data repre- sentative of farm operations in 2012. The results of this study made significant contributions to the development of the sustainability goals outlined in Egg Farmers of Canada's inaugural sustainability report (Egg Farmers of Canada, 2020b), and were consistent with previous LCA studies of egg production in identifying feed inputs as a key driver of environmental impacts. Importantly, however, this study used data collected prior to the initiation of transition away from conventional production in Canada. In their study of Dutch egg production systems, Dekker al. (2011) hypothesize that a ban on conventional cages would increase cumulative environmental impacts of egg production. This underscores the importance of continuous assessment of the Canadian egg industry as it navigates this transition, in order to provide bench- mark targets, and identify priority strategies for improving environ- mental sustainability outcomes of Canadian egg production. On this basis, the research presented here makes five key updates to the work done by Pelletier (2017): use of a greatly expanded data set represen- tative of a larger proportion of Canadian egg production capacity; use of data more recent in temporal scope (i.e. from 2019); differentiation of life cycle inventories and impact assessment results for both single- and multi-tier free run production systems; expansion of the number of environmental impacts considered; and performance of quantitative uncertainty assessment, in line with best practices in the LCA field (Bamber et al., 2020).

7: A survey was used to collect data from farmers regarding housing system type and characteristics, management practices, on farm resource use, and hen performance. A copy of the survey may be found on the Open Science Framework at https://osf.io/xrckz/?show=view. Housing systems were defined in accordance with Canadian standards (National Farm Animal Care Council, 2017). Enriched cages provide a larger space allowance per bird than conventional cages, as well as ac- cess to different furnishings and enrichments within the cage, including perches, nest boxes, scratch areas, etc. Non-cage systems include both single-tier and multi-tier systems. In these systems, hens live either directly on the floor or within an aviary structure, with access to

perches, nest and scratch areas, and other enrichments. The amount of access to different furnishing and enrichments may differ from farm to farm, above minimum thresholds (National Farm Animal Care Council, 2017). A free-range system is defined as either a single- or multi-tier system in which hens are provided with outdoor access. Certified organic production systems may also be either single- or multi-tier systems that adhere to additional requirements for production (for example exposure to natural light) set by the Canadian General Stan- dards Board (CGSB) (CGSB, 2015).

Only commercial egg farms operating under the Canadian supply management system were included in the study. A description of the Canadian egg industry, including the supply management system, is available in Pelletier (2017). Survey responses were collected in two ways. First, responses were collected via in-person interviews with farm owners/managers. Commercial laying hen farmers were recruited for voluntary participation through their respective provincial egg boards. Once recruited, producers were instructed to answer survey questions with respect to their most recently completed flock. In instances where farmers had multiple flocks operating simultaneously, they were instructed to complete survey questions with respect to their largest flock. In instances in which a producer operated a split flock (i.e. a single barn divided into two sides each housing an independent flock), each side was treated as a separate, distinct barn; as such farmers were asked to answer survey questions only with respect to the most recently completed side of the barn. Once a single survey was completed, farmers operating multiple flocks were invited to fill out additional surveys for other flocks at their discretion.

Second, the survey was made available online using the University of British Columbia survey tool provided by Qualtrics (2005). Access links to the survey (both English and French versions) were provided to the provincial egg boards for distribution to commercial egg farmers elec- tronically during Fall 2019. All instructions for answering survey questions were the same for online survey responses as they were for in-person responses.

In addition, to increase sample sizes, data collected during Summer and Fall 2019 was supplemented with data previously collected in 2017 as part of a collaborative effort between Egg Farmers of Canada and the World Wildlife Fund. The surveys used for each of these data collection activities shared largely the same questions, with some minor differ- ences. Relevant information from this supplementary data source was incorporated alongside the data collected in 2019 for further analysis.

All data, which were collected under guarantee of non-disclosure with respect to individual facilities, were coded to ensure confidenti- ality for individual producers. All useable information was extracted from both complete and incomplete surveys. In cases where further clarification was necessary or to fill in missing values or correct obvious errors, follow ups were conducted if farmers had indicated their consent and provided contact information when filling out the survey. When follow ups were unsuccessful obvious errors were excluded for that particular variable, and invalid responses were considered to be missing values. Importantly, errors or missing responses in one survey question did not necessitate complete exclusion of the entire survey.

The surveys used for each of these data collection activities shared largely the same questions, with some minor differ- ences. Relevant information from this supplementary data source was incorporated alongside the data collected in 2019 for further analysis.

All data, which were collected under guarantee of non-disclosure with respect to individual facilities, were coded to ensure confidenti- ality for individual producers. All useable information was extracted from both complete and incomplete surveys. In cases where further clarification was necessary or to fill in missing values or correct obvious errors, follow ups were conducted if farmers had indicated their consent and provided contact information when filling out the survey. When follow ups were unsuccessful obvious errors were excluded for that particular variable, and invalid responses were considered to be missing values. Importantly, errors or missing responses in one survey question did not necessitate complete exclusion of the entire survey.
11: A survey was used to collect data from farmers regarding housing system type and characteristics, management practices, on farm resource use, and hen performance. A copy of the survey may be found on the Open Science Framework at https://osf.io/xrckz/?show=view. Housing systems were defined in accordance with Canadian standards (National Farm Animal Care Council, 2017). Enriched cages provide a larger space allowance per bird than conventional cages, as well as ac- cess to different furnishings and enrichments within the cage, including perches, nest boxes, scratch areas, etc. Non-cage systems include both single-tier and multi-tier systems. In these systems, hens live either directly on the floor or within an aviary structure, with access to

perches, nest and scratch areas, and other enrichments. The amount of access to different furnishing and enrichments may differ from farm to farm, above minimum thresholds (National Farm Animal Care Council, 2017). A free-range system is defined as either a single- or multi-tier system in which hens are provided with outdoor access. Certified organic production systems may also be either single- or multi-tier systems that adhere to additional requirements for production (for example exposure to natural light) set by the Canadian General Stan- dards Board (CGSB) (CGSB, 2015).

Only commercial egg farms operating under the Canadian supply management system were included in the study. A description of the Canadian egg industry, including the supply management system, is available in Pelletier (2017). Survey responses were collected in two ways. First, responses were collected via in-person interviews with farm owners/managers. Commercial laying hen farmers were recruited for voluntary participation through their respective provincial egg boards. Once recruited, producers were instructed to answer survey questions with respect to their most recently completed flock. In instances where farmers had multiple flocks operating simultaneously, they were instructed to complete survey questions with respect to their largest flock. In instances in which a producer operated a split flock (i.e. a single barn divided into two sides each housing an independent flock), each side was treated as a separate, distinct barn; as such farmers were asked to answer survey questions only with respect to the most recently completed side of the barn. Once a single survey was completed, farmers operating multiple flocks were invited to fill out additional surveys for other flocks at their discretion.

Second, the survey was made available online using the University of British Columbia survey tool provided by Qualtrics (2005). Access links to the survey (both English and French versions) were provided to the provincial egg boards for distribution to commercial egg farmers elec- tronically during Fall 2019. All instructions for answering survey questions were the same for online survey responses as they were for in-person responses.

In addition, to increase sample sizes, data collected during Summer and Fall 2019 was supplemented with data previously collected in 2017 as part of a collaborative effort between Egg Farmers of Canada and the World Wildlife Fund. The surveys used for each of these data collection activities shared largely the same questions, with some minor differ- ences. Relevant information from this supplementary data source was incorporated alongside the data collected in 2019 for further analysis.

All data, which were collected under guarantee of non-disclosure with respect to individual facilities, were coded to ensure confidenti- ality for individual producers. All useable information was extracted from both complete and incomplete surveys. In cases where further clarification was necessary or to fill in missing values or correct obvious errors, follow ups were conducted if farmers had indicated their consent and provided contact information when filling out the survey. When follow ups were unsuccessful obvious errors were excluded for that particular variable, and invalid responses were considered to be missing values. Importantly, errors or missing responses in one survey question did not necessitate complete exclusion of the entire survey.
12: 2.2.1.1. Objectives. The goal of this study was to perform an ISO 14044- compliant (ISO, 2006a) cradle-to-farm gate attributional environmental LCA of the Canadian egg industry, differentiated by housing system. Previously, Pelletier (2017) performed a similar assessment using cradle-to-customer system boundaries, and data collected representing 2012 flocks. The current study updates this baseline, national-level LCA using data collected during 2017, and Summer and Fall 2019, and compares the life cycle impacts of Canadian egg production systems to

2.2.1.2. System boundaries and functional unit. The system boundaries for this study include all relevant material, energy, and emission flows linked to all processes in the cradle-to-farm gate egg supply chain in Canada (Fig. 1). These include breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, and layer facilities. Only egg production facilities and management of manure produced during egg production were included in the foreground pro- cesses. All other processes were considered part of the background system and were based on the 2012 Canadian baseline model (Pelletier, 2017). Transportation between processes is also considered. The func- tional unit is one tonne of eggs at the farm gate. The functional unit and system boundaries have been chosen in line with those used in Pelletier (2017) to ensure comparisons between results can be made on a meth- odologically consistent basis.

2.2.1.3. Co-product allocation. Allocation between coproducts (i.e. eggs and spent hens at the end of the egg production process) was performed on the basis of gross chemical energy content. This was chosen as an appropriate physical allocation criteria as it more accurately reflects the proportions of biological energy dedicated to production of eggs and meat by hens. A detailed discussion of this principle is available in Pelletier and Tyedmers (2011) and Pelletier et al. (2015). Coproducts were defined as those products that are used in some further economic activity. Spent hens used for human consumption or rendering for ani- mal feeds were allocated to, while those buried, composted, incinerated, or otherwise disposed of as waste were not. Allocation factors were calculated based on an assumed caloric density of 1520 kcal/kg for eggs (Egg Farmers of Canada, 2018), and 1906.43 kcal/kg for spent hens (Pelletier, 2017). Default background allocation factors were used on a mass basis.

2.2.1.4. Cut-off criteria and exclusions. Inputs and emissions associated with production and maintenance of infrastructure were excluded as they generally make small contributions to total environmental impacts (i.e. <5%), which decrease further when amortized against total pro- duction over their life-span (roughly 30 years for a layer barn) (Li, 2020). Feed inputs contributing less than 0.1% of the national, production-weighted average feed composition for layers feeds were excluded. A similar cut off threshold was used when modelling feed formulations for breeder and pullet flocks, unless the feed input had already been modelled as part of the layer feed formulation. Antibiotics, cleaners, and poultry enteric fermentation were also excluded in calculation of life cycle emissions due to their negligible relevance.
Land use changes, tillage regimes, and soil amendments related to

production of agricultural feed stocks and livestock for use in feed for- mulations may have large impacts on soil organic carbon and CO2 sequestration (Levasseur et al., 2012; Regina and Alakukku, 2010; Xu et al., 2019). However, these impacts were also excluded due to high levels of uncertainty in available modeling methodologies regarding rates of carbon sequestration/loss, and longevity of sequestration effects (Goglio et al., 2015). In addition, irrigation water was excluded as previous analyses of systems producing feed inputs included in this study suggest very limited irrigation inputs (AFSC, 2020; Pelletier, 2017; StatsCan, 2013).

2.2.2.6.0. Egg production data. Foreground data for egg production was collected via survey as previously described. Separate production weighted averages were calculated for each non-energy input based on a four-step process. All eggs across each housing system were assumed to have an average mass of 60g. This mass represents a midpoint between "large" and "extra large" sized eggs, rounded up, as defined by the Ca- nadian Food Inspection Agency (Canadian Food Inspection Agency, 2021).

Since all calculations were production weighted, all those survey responses which did not provide information on production were removed. Next, all responses were scaled to a one-year long (i.e. 365 day) laying cycle in order to account for variability in cycle length be- tween flocks (average length: 365.4 I 14.11 days; maximum: 434 days; minimum: 308 days). After scaling, production outliers were informally identified in accordance with Laurikkala et al. (2000). Using this method, a data point was identified as an outlier if the value was greater than quartile 3 + 1.5 X interquartile range, or less than quartile 1 1.5 X interquartile range. Dozens of eggs produced per hen per year was chosen as the metric for outlier detection to account for variability in production due to differences in flock sizes between surveys. Production outliers were detected individually for different housing systems to ac- count for variability in production across systems. If a survey response was found to be a production outlier it was removed completely. Following removal of production outliers, a second stage of outlier identification was performed using the same method for each of the individual inputs. If a survey was found to be an outlier for a specific input, or it did not provide a response for that input, it was removed from calculation of the production weighted average. It is important to note that this did not preclude other values from that data set being used in calculation of other production weighted averages. Outlier detection and calculation of production weighted averages
Fertilizers, pesticides, energy carriers, packaging, transportation, etc.
Fig. 1. System boundaries for the cradle-to-farm gate environmental LCA of the Canadian egg supply chain.

for energy inputs was difficult due to low response rates and large variability in reported energy consumption across farms. Energy con- sumption data was collected for electricity, diesel, gasoline, liquified petroleum gas/propane, fuel oil, and natural gas. Across every barn surveyed, electricity was the only consistently used type of energy for lighting, ventilation, and other automated systems. Since electricity is consistently used to some degree in all barns, data sets for which elec- tricity consumption data were not provided were removed in calculation of production weighted electricity consumption. In some cases, farmers reported electricity consumption in terms of the amount of money spent during that production period. When this was the case, consumption was calculated based on average electricity prices in each province as re- ported by Urban (2020).

Outliers for electricity consumption were identified through com- parison of calculated values with the ranges reported by Pelletier (2017, supplementary tables S16-20). If a value fell outside of the previously reported range, it was identified as an outlier and removed. This method of outlier detection was not applied to electricity consumption for either single- or multi-tier free run barns because the range previously reported by Pelletier (2017, supplementary table S18) did not differentiate be- tween different free run systems, and because the reported range was much narrower than that reported for any other system. As such, only obvious outliers identified via visual inspection were removed to pre- serve the variability of the data sets.

In contrast to electricity, other forms of energy are variously used across farms for processes such as heating, running backup generators, barn cleaning, etc. Since the other sources of energy covered by the survey are not consistently applied across all farms, if a farm did not report consumption levels for a particular fuel it was assumed to be zero. These zeroes were subsequently included in calculation of production weighted average consumptions. Outlier detection could not be per- formed on these data due to the inconsistent use of these energy sources, the inability to differentiate between zero and missing values, and the large variation seen in reported energy consumption levels across those farms that did report consumption. As such, only obvious outliers on the very high end of the spectrum that could be identified visually were removed.

In some cases, insufficient data was collected for calculation of production weighted averages for non-electricity energy sources. When this was the case, the average values presented in Pelletier (2017, sup- plementary tables S15-20) were used. Specifically, the following values reported by Pelletier (2017) were used: all non-electricity energy inputs for single-tier free run production; and all non-electricity energy inputs for multi-tier free run, free range, and organic production with the exception of diesel. No updated data were calculated for transportation values. As such, these values are the same as those reported by Pelletier (2017). The values reported for free run production were applied to both single- and multi-tier free run models. Conversion factors used for calculating energy consumption where necessary are presented in sup- plementary table S1. Provincial electricity models as well as models for all other energy inputs were taken from the ecoInvent database, (Frischknecht al., 2005).

2.2.2.7.0. Modeling N, P, and methane emissions from poultry man- ure. Actual manure removal amount data were not collected, as previ- ous efforts to collect estimates of manure volumes from farmers resulted in a data set of low resolution and quality (Pelletier 2017). A standard manure excretion rate was assumed, scaled relative to the feed conver- sion efficiencies calculated for each housing system (Pelletier, 2017; Pelletier et al., 2014). Importantly, this excretion rate did not take into account the impact of hen genetics, an important factor in determining feed conversion efficiency (Yuan et al., 2017, 2015). Nitrogen and phosphorous excretion rates were calculated based on a nutrient mass balance model using feed composition and assuming that 2.2% of hen body mass is N and 0.6% is P, and that eggs contain 1.7% N and 0.21% P in accordance with Koelsch (2007). From the estimated P excretion, P emissions were calculated assuming a 2.9% run-off rate at time of

application of manure to agricultural lands, in line with Dalgaard et al. (2008). Estimates of N excretion were subsequently used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia, and nitric oxide emissions from manure management, and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions in accordance with IPCC (2006) Tier 2 pro- tocols. Default emission factors for emissions at time of deposition, storage, and application were used. Calculation of nitrogenous emis- sions accounted for the proportions of different manure management systems used across each housing system (i.e. covered/uncovered dry and liquid storage, and direct field application without storage) through the calculation of production weighted average emission factors. A ni- trate leaching rate of 20% was assumed for calculation of N losses due to leaching during manure storage for uncovered manure storage, and 0% for covered manure storage. Methane emissions from manure manage- ment were calculated in accordance with IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols, taking into account the proportions and residency time of manure in different storage systems. Methane emissions factors for layers were 0.03 and 1.2 kg CH4 /head/year for hens in dry and wet manure man- agement systems respectively. Field level methane emissions resulting from direct application of manure to agricultural fields were not included, as no such corresponding emissions factors could be found in the IPCC (2006) methods.

2.2.2.8.0. Modeling feed input supply chains. Background data for feed supply chains was collected from several secondary sources. Energy inputs for feed milling processes were derived from LCA studies of the US egg industry (Pelletier, Ibarburu, & Xin, 2013; Pelletier et al., 2014), as in Pelletier (2017). Feed formulations for breeder flock, pullet, and layer facilities are representative of production-weighted average com- positions based on producer-reported data collected by Pelletier (2017). These formulations, in turn, dictated, material inputs to feed milling processes. Canadian-average models were used for production of wheat, corn, and soy, adjusted to represent appropriate input levels of fertilizer and pesticides, seed, and energy inputs, as well as yields. Models for barley production were developed based on input data per tonne taken from (S&T)2 (2008), adjusted to reflect 2012 Canadian average yields of 2.9 tonnes/ha as reported by StatsCan. Models for canola production were developed based on input data per tonne from Cheminfo and (S&T)2 (2013), adjusted to reflect 2012 Canadian average yields of 1.6 tonnes/ha as reported by StatsCan. Inventory data representing organic corn, wheat, soy, and canola production for use in organic feed milling processes were taken from Pelletier et al. (2008a). Field level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitrate, and carbon dioxide emissions associated with field crop production were calculated according to IPCC Tier 1 protocols (2006) using default emission factors. Phosphate emissions from run-off were assumed to be 2.9% following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizer and pesticide inputs were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, while seed inputs were assumed to be transported 100 km by truck. Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were used, with inventory data models for individual fertilizer inputs taken from the EcoInvent database (Frischknecht et al., 2005). These models are representative of average production conditions across Europe.

In addition, peas also make significant contributions to the modelled feed formulations. Models representing the production of peas were the only feed crop production model that differed from those used in Pel- letier (2017). Rather, a prairie (i.e. Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Man- itoba) average pea production model was used (Dutta et al., 2020). This model was developed using production-weighted averages based on approximately 300 surveys of Canadian pea farmers across the prairie provinces, which collected data on field level inputs, activities, and production. Field level emissions associated with pea production were modelled using the process-based DNDC model, adjusted for Canadian feed crop production (Li et al., 1992).

Many livestock derived feed ingredients were also included in the modeled feed formulations, including fat and meat/bone meal products rendered from cattle, swine, and poultry processing co-products, as well as poultry feather meal. Models representing beef and swine production

were adapted from models of US production systems (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with changes made to represent Canadian production conditions. Models for ruminant and porcine products are derived from Pelletier et al. (2014). Inventory models for other inputs to feed pro- duction including sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids, and other pre-mixes and supplements were derived from the EcoInvent database (2005), with all mineral inputs modeled as salt. The amino acid DL-methionine was used as a proxy for all amino acid inputs, and was modeled using inventory data as reported by Binder (2003). Other pre-mixes and supplements were excluded from feed models due to their wide variability in composition, and the lack of detailed composition data.

2.2.2.9.0. Data quality assessment. In accordance with ISO 14044 (2006b), data quality was assessed for foreground (i.e. egg production and layer manure management) processes, as well as background pro- cesses from Pelletier (2017). Background processes for which data quality was assessed include pullet, hatchery, and breeder flocks and associated manure management activities, feed milling processes for layer, pullet, and breeder flocks, and feed crop production models (Fig. 1). Data quality was assessed for each flow into and out of these processes using the default EcoInvent pedigree matrix, as described by Table 1 in Ciroth et al. (2016). Specifically, data quality was assessed in regards to reliability, completeness, and temporal, geographical, and technological correlation. Importantly, data quality assessment of this nature includes a degree of subjectivity. For each flow assessed, data quality scores were defined in relation to how well the data represented by the flow actually represented the modeling goal for each flow. Data quality was not assessed for background processes from LCI databases.

2.2.2.10.0. Data uncertainty assessment. Based on recommended best practices for uncertainty assessment in LCA (Bamber et al., 2019), parameter uncertainty (i.e. uncertainty in inventory data) was propa- gated using Monte Carlo simulations with 1000 runs. Where feasible, uncertainty factors for egg production were based on a combination of calculated geometric standard deviations, and uncertainty contributions from the pedigree matrix entries, as defined in Ciroth et al. (2016). Null values were not taken into account during calculation of geometric standard deviations for energy flows. In some cases, small sample sizes resulted in calculation of uncertainty factors several times higher than what would be expected for the agricultural sector, as noted by Frisch- knecht et al. (2005). Because of this, calculated uncertainty values greater than 5 were removed and replaced with the corresponding generic, sector-specific uncertainty value from Frischknecht et al. (2005). Five was chosen as an upper bound because it is the highest possible base uncertainty value defined by Frischknecht et al. (2005). Neither uncertainty due to characterization models, nor due to meth- odological choices were assessed.

Phosphate emissions from run-off were assumed to be 2.9% following Dalgaard et al. (2008). Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were used, with inventory data models for individual fertilizer inputs taken from the EcoInvent database (Frischknecht et al., 2005). These models are representative of average production conditions across Europe.

In addition, peas also make significant contributions to the modelled feed formulations. Models representing the production of peas were the only feed crop production model that differed from those used in Pel- letier (2017). Rather, a prairie (i.e. Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Man- itoba) average pea production model was used (Dutta et al., 2020). This model was developed using production-weighted averages based on approximately 300 surveys of Canadian pea farmers across the prairie provinces, which collected data on field level inputs, activities, and production. Field level emissions associated with pea production were modelled using the process-based DNDC model, adjusted for Canadian feed crop production (Li et al., 1992).

Many livestock derived feed ingredients were also included in the modeled feed formulations, including fat and meat/bone meal products rendered from cattle, swine, and poultry processing co-products, as well as poultry feather meal. Models representing beef and swine production

were adapted from models of US production systems (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with changes made to represent Canadian production conditions. Models for ruminant and porcine products are derived from Pelletier et al. (2014). Inventory models for other inputs to feed pro- duction including sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids, and other pre-mixes and supplements were derived from the EcoInvent database (2005), with all mineral inputs modeled as salt. The amino acid DL-methionine was used as a proxy for all amino acid inputs, and was modeled using inventory data as reported by Binder (2003). Other pre-mixes and supplements were excluded from feed models due to their wide variability in composition, and the lack of detailed composition data.

2.2.2.9.0. Data quality assessment. In accordance with ISO 14044 (2006b), data quality was assessed for foreground (i.e. egg production and layer manure management) processes, as well as background pro- cesses from Pelletier (2017). Background processes for which data quality was assessed include pullet, hatchery, and breeder flocks and associated manure management activities, feed milling processes for layer, pullet, and breeder flocks, and feed crop production models (Fig. 1). Data quality was assessed for each flow into and out of these processes using the default EcoInvent pedigree matrix, as described by Table 1 in Ciroth et al. (2016). Specifically, data quality was assessed in regards to reliability, completeness, and temporal, geographical, and technological correlation. Importantly, data quality assessment of this nature includes a degree of subjectivity. For each flow assessed, data quality scores were defined in relation to how well the data represented by the flow actually represented the modeling goal for each flow. Data quality was not assessed for background processes from LCI databases.

2.2.2.10.0. Data uncertainty assessment. Based on recommended best practices for uncertainty assessment in LCA (Bamber et al., 2019), parameter uncertainty (i.e. uncertainty in inventory data) was propa- gated using Monte Carlo simulations with 1000 runs. Where feasible, uncertainty factors for egg production were based on a combination of calculated geometric standard deviations, and uncertainty contributions from the pedigree matrix entries, as defined in Ciroth et al. (2016). Null values were not taken into account during calculation of geometric standard deviations for energy flows. In some cases, small sample sizes resulted in calculation of uncertainty factors several times higher than what would be expected for the agricultural sector, as noted by Frisch- knecht et al. (2005). Because of this, calculated uncertainty values greater than 5 were removed and replaced with the corresponding generic, sector-specific uncertainty value from Frischknecht et al. (2005). Five was chosen as an upper bound because it is the highest possible base uncertainty value defined by Frischknecht et al. (2005). Neither uncertainty due to characterization models, nor due to meth- odological choices were assessed.
14: 2.2.1.1. Objectives. The goal of this study was to perform an ISO 14044- compliant (ISO, 2006a) cradle-to-farm gate attributional environmental LCA of the Canadian egg industry, differentiated by housing system. Previously, Pelletier (2017) performed a similar assessment using cradle-to-customer system boundaries, and data collected representing 2012 flocks. The current study updates this baseline, national-level LCA using data collected during 2017, and Summer and Fall 2019, and compares the life cycle impacts of Canadian egg production systems to

2.2.1.2. System boundaries and functional unit. The system boundaries for this study include all relevant material, energy, and emission flows linked to all processes in the cradle-to-farm gate egg supply chain in Canada (Fig. 1). These include breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, and layer facilities. Only egg production facilities and management of manure produced during egg production were included in the foreground pro- cesses. All other processes were considered part of the background system and were based on the 2012 Canadian baseline model (Pelletier, 2017). Transportation between processes is also considered. The func- tional unit is one tonne of eggs at the farm gate. The functional unit and system boundaries have been chosen in line with those used in Pelletier (2017) to ensure comparisons between results can be made on a meth- odologically consistent basis.

2.2.1.3. Co-product allocation. Allocation between coproducts (i.e. eggs and spent hens at the end of the egg production process) was performed on the basis of gross chemical energy content. This was chosen as an appropriate physical allocation criteria as it more accurately reflects the proportions of biological energy dedicated to production of eggs and meat by hens. A detailed discussion of this principle is available in Pelletier and Tyedmers (2011) and Pelletier et al. (2015). Coproducts were defined as those products that are used in some further economic activity. Spent hens used for human consumption or rendering for ani- mal feeds were allocated to, while those buried, composted, incinerated, or otherwise disposed of as waste were not. Allocation factors were calculated based on an assumed caloric density of 1520 kcal/kg for eggs (Egg Farmers of Canada, 2018), and 1906.43 kcal/kg for spent hens (Pelletier, 2017). Default background allocation factors were used on a mass basis.

2.2.1.4. Cut-off criteria and exclusions. Inputs and emissions associated with production and maintenance of infrastructure were excluded as they generally make small contributions to total environmental impacts (i.e. <5%), which decrease further when amortized against total pro- duction over their life-span (roughly 30 years for a layer barn) (Li, 2020). Feed inputs contributing less than 0.1% of the national, production-weighted average feed composition for layers feeds were excluded. A similar cut off threshold was used when modelling feed formulations for breeder and pullet flocks, unless the feed input had already been modelled as part of the layer feed formulation. Antibiotics, cleaners, and poultry enteric fermentation were also excluded in calculation of life cycle emissions due to their negligible relevance.
Land use changes, tillage regimes, and soil amendments related to

production of agricultural feed stocks and livestock for use in feed for- mulations may have large impacts on soil organic carbon and CO2 sequestration (Levasseur et al., 2012; Regina and Alakukku, 2010; Xu et al., 2019). However, these impacts were also excluded due to high levels of uncertainty in available modeling methodologies regarding rates of carbon sequestration/loss, and longevity of sequestration effects (Goglio et al., 2015). In addition, irrigation water was excluded as previous analyses of systems producing feed inputs included in this study suggest very limited irrigation inputs (AFSC, 2020; Pelletier, 2017; StatsCan, 2013).

2.2.2.6.0. Egg production data. Foreground data for egg production was collected via survey as previously described. Separate production weighted averages were calculated for each non-energy input based on a four-step process. All eggs across each housing system were assumed to have an average mass of 60g. This mass represents a midpoint between "large" and "extra large" sized eggs, rounded up, as defined by the Ca- nadian Food Inspection Agency (Canadian Food Inspection Agency, 2021).

Since all calculations were production weighted, all those survey responses which did not provide information on production were removed. Next, all responses were scaled to a one-year long (i.e. 365 day) laying cycle in order to account for variability in cycle length be- tween flocks (average length: 365.4 I 14.11 days; maximum: 434 days; minimum: 308 days). After scaling, production outliers were informally identified in accordance with Laurikkala et al. (2000). Using this method, a data point was identified as an outlier if the value was greater than quartile 3 + 1.5 X interquartile range, or less than quartile 1 1.5 X interquartile range. Dozens of eggs produced per hen per year was chosen as the metric for outlier detection to account for variability in production due to differences in flock sizes between surveys. Production outliers were detected individually for different housing systems to ac- count for variability in production across systems. If a survey response was found to be a production outlier it was removed completely. Following removal of production outliers, a second stage of outlier identification was performed using the same method for each of the individual inputs. If a survey was found to be an outlier for a specific input, or it did not provide a response for that input, it was removed from calculation of the production weighted average. It is important to note that this did not preclude other values from that data set being used in calculation of other production weighted averages. Outlier detection and calculation of production weighted averages
Fertilizers, pesticides, energy carriers, packaging, transportation, etc.
Fig. 1. System boundaries for the cradle-to-farm gate environmental LCA of the Canadian egg supply chain.

for energy inputs was difficult due to low response rates and large variability in reported energy consumption across farms. Energy con- sumption data was collected for electricity, diesel, gasoline, liquified petroleum gas/propane, fuel oil, and natural gas. Across every barn surveyed, electricity was the only consistently used type of energy for lighting, ventilation, and other automated systems. Since electricity is consistently used to some degree in all barns, data sets for which elec- tricity consumption data were not provided were removed in calculation of production weighted electricity consumption. In some cases, farmers reported electricity consumption in terms of the amount of money spent during that production period. When this was the case, consumption was calculated based on average electricity prices in each province as re- ported by Urban (2020).

Outliers for electricity consumption were identified through com- parison of calculated values with the ranges reported by Pelletier (2017, supplementary tables S16-20). If a value fell outside of the previously reported range, it was identified as an outlier and removed. This method of outlier detection was not applied to electricity consumption for either single- or multi-tier free run barns because the range previously reported by Pelletier (2017, supplementary table S18) did not differentiate be- tween different free run systems, and because the reported range was much narrower than that reported for any other system. As such, only obvious outliers identified via visual inspection were removed to pre- serve the variability of the data sets.

In contrast to electricity, other forms of energy are variously used across farms for processes such as heating, running backup generators, barn cleaning, etc. Since the other sources of energy covered by the survey are not consistently applied across all farms, if a farm did not report consumption levels for a particular fuel it was assumed to be zero. These zeroes were subsequently included in calculation of production weighted average consumptions. Outlier detection could not be per- formed on these data due to the inconsistent use of these energy sources, the inability to differentiate between zero and missing values, and the large variation seen in reported energy consumption levels across those farms that did report consumption. As such, only obvious outliers on the very high end of the spectrum that could be identified visually were removed.

In some cases, insufficient data was collected for calculation of production weighted averages for non-electricity energy sources. When this was the case, the average values presented in Pelletier (2017, sup- plementary tables S15-20) were used. Specifically, the following values reported by Pelletier (2017) were used: all non-electricity energy inputs for single-tier free run production; and all non-electricity energy inputs for multi-tier free run, free range, and organic production with the exception of diesel. No updated data were calculated for transportation values. As such, these values are the same as those reported by Pelletier (2017). The values reported for free run production were applied to both single- and multi-tier free run models. Conversion factors used for calculating energy consumption where necessary are presented in sup- plementary table S1. Provincial electricity models as well as models for all other energy inputs were taken from the ecoInvent database, (Frischknecht al., 2005).

2.2.2.7.0. Modeling N, P, and methane emissions from poultry man- ure. Actual manure removal amount data were not collected, as previ- ous efforts to collect estimates of manure volumes from farmers resulted in a data set of low resolution and quality (Pelletier 2017). A standard manure excretion rate was assumed, scaled relative to the feed conver- sion efficiencies calculated for each housing system (Pelletier, 2017; Pelletier et al., 2014). Importantly, this excretion rate did not take into account the impact of hen genetics, an important factor in determining feed conversion efficiency (Yuan et al., 2017, 2015). Nitrogen and phosphorous excretion rates were calculated based on a nutrient mass balance model using feed composition and assuming that 2.2% of hen body mass is N and 0.6% is P, and that eggs contain 1.7% N and 0.21% P in accordance with Koelsch (2007). From the estimated P excretion, P emissions were calculated assuming a 2.9% run-off rate at time of

application of manure to agricultural lands, in line with Dalgaard et al. (2008). Estimates of N excretion were subsequently used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia, and nitric oxide emissions from manure management, and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions in accordance with IPCC (2006) Tier 2 pro- tocols. Default emission factors for emissions at time of deposition, storage, and application were used. Calculation of nitrogenous emis- sions accounted for the proportions of different manure management systems used across each housing system (i.e. covered/uncovered dry and liquid storage, and direct field application without storage) through the calculation of production weighted average emission factors. A ni- trate leaching rate of 20% was assumed for calculation of N losses due to leaching during manure storage for uncovered manure storage, and 0% for covered manure storage. Methane emissions from manure manage- ment were calculated in accordance with IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols, taking into account the proportions and residency time of manure in different storage systems. Methane emissions factors for layers were 0.03 and 1.2 kg CH4 /head/year for hens in dry and wet manure man- agement systems respectively. Field level methane emissions resulting from direct application of manure to agricultural fields were not included, as no such corresponding emissions factors could be found in the IPCC (2006) methods.

2.2.2.8.0. Modeling feed input supply chains. Background data for feed supply chains was collected from several secondary sources. Energy inputs for feed milling processes were derived from LCA studies of the US egg industry (Pelletier, Ibarburu, & Xin, 2013; Pelletier et al., 2014), as in Pelletier (2017). Feed formulations for breeder flock, pullet, and layer facilities are representative of production-weighted average com- positions based on producer-reported data collected by Pelletier (2017). These formulations, in turn, dictated, material inputs to feed milling processes. Canadian-average models were used for production of wheat, corn, and soy, adjusted to represent appropriate input levels of fertilizer and pesticides, seed, and energy inputs, as well as yields. Models for barley production were developed based on input data per tonne taken from (S&T)2 (2008), adjusted to reflect 2012 Canadian average yields of 2.9 tonnes/ha as reported by StatsCan. Models for canola production were developed based on input data per tonne from Cheminfo and (S&T)2 (2013), adjusted to reflect 2012 Canadian average yields of 1.6 tonnes/ha as reported by StatsCan. Inventory data representing organic corn, wheat, soy, and canola production for use in organic feed milling processes were taken from Pelletier et al. (2008a). Field level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitrate, and carbon dioxide emissions associated with field crop production were calculated according to IPCC Tier 1 protocols (2006) using default emission factors. Phosphate emissions from run-off were assumed to be 2.9% following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizer and pesticide inputs were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, while seed inputs were assumed to be transported 100 km by truck. Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were used, with inventory data models for individual fertilizer inputs taken from the EcoInvent database (Frischknecht et al., 2005). These models are representative of average production conditions across Europe.

In addition, peas also make significant contributions to the modelled feed formulations. Models representing the production of peas were the only feed crop production model that differed from those used in Pel- letier (2017). Rather, a prairie (i.e. Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Man- itoba) average pea production model was used (Dutta et al., 2020). This model was developed using production-weighted averages based on approximately 300 surveys of Canadian pea farmers across the prairie provinces, which collected data on field level inputs, activities, and production. Field level emissions associated with pea production were modelled using the process-based DNDC model, adjusted for Canadian feed crop production (Li et al., 1992).

Many livestock derived feed ingredients were also included in the modeled feed formulations, including fat and meat/bone meal products rendered from cattle, swine, and poultry processing co-products, as well as poultry feather meal. Models representing beef and swine production

were adapted from models of US production systems (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with changes made to represent Canadian production conditions. Models for ruminant and porcine products are derived from Pelletier et al. (2014). Inventory models for other inputs to feed pro- duction including sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids, and other pre-mixes and supplements were derived from the EcoInvent database (2005), with all mineral inputs modeled as salt. The amino acid DL-methionine was used as a proxy for all amino acid inputs, and was modeled using inventory data as reported by Binder (2003). Other pre-mixes and supplements were excluded from feed models due to their wide variability in composition, and the lack of detailed composition data.

2.2.2.9.0. Data quality assessment. In accordance with ISO 14044 (2006b), data quality was assessed for foreground (i.e. egg production and layer manure management) processes, as well as background pro- cesses from Pelletier (2017). Background processes for which data quality was assessed include pullet, hatchery, and breeder flocks and associated manure management activities, feed milling processes for layer, pullet, and breeder flocks, and feed crop production models (Fig. 1). Data quality was assessed for each flow into and out of these processes using the default EcoInvent pedigree matrix, as described by Table 1 in Ciroth et al. (2016). Specifically, data quality was assessed in regards to reliability, completeness, and temporal, geographical, and technological correlation. Importantly, data quality assessment of this nature includes a degree of subjectivity. For each flow assessed, data quality scores were defined in relation to how well the data represented by the flow actually represented the modeling goal for each flow. Data quality was not assessed for background processes from LCI databases.

2.2.2.10.0. Data uncertainty assessment. Based on recommended best practices for uncertainty assessment in LCA (Bamber et al., 2019), parameter uncertainty (i.e. uncertainty in inventory data) was propa- gated using Monte Carlo simulations with 1000 runs. Where feasible, uncertainty factors for egg production were based on a combination of calculated geometric standard deviations, and uncertainty contributions from the pedigree matrix entries, as defined in Ciroth et al. (2016). Null values were not taken into account during calculation of geometric standard deviations for energy flows. In some cases, small sample sizes resulted in calculation of uncertainty factors several times higher than what would be expected for the agricultural sector, as noted by Frisch- knecht et al. (2005). Because of this, calculated uncertainty values greater than 5 were removed and replaced with the corresponding generic, sector-specific uncertainty value from Frischknecht et al. (2005). Five was chosen as an upper bound because it is the highest possible base uncertainty value defined by Frischknecht et al. (2005). Neither uncertainty due to characterization models, nor due to meth- odological choices were assessed.
