Specifically, data quality was assessed in regards to reliability, completeness, and temporal, geographical, and technological correlation. Importantly, data quality assessment of this nature includes a degree of subjectivity. For each flow assessed, data quality scores were defined in relation to how well the data represented by the flow actually represented the modeling goal for each flow. Data quality was not assessed for background processes from LCI databases.

2.2.2.10.0. Data uncertainty assessment. Based on recommended best practices for uncertainty assessment in LCA (Bamber et al., 2019), parameter uncertainty (i.e. uncertainty in inventory data) was propa- gated using Monte Carlo simulations with 1000 runs. Where feasible, uncertainty factors for egg production were based on a combination of calculated geometric standard deviations, and uncertainty contributions from the pedigree matrix entries, as defined in Ciroth et al. (2016). Null values were not taken into account during calculation of geometric standard deviations for energy flows. In some cases, small sample sizes resulted in calculation of uncertainty factors several times higher than what would be expected for the agricultural sector, as noted by Frisch- knecht et al. (2005). Because of this, calculated uncertainty values greater than 5 were removed and replaced with the corresponding generic, sector-specific uncertainty value from Frischknecht et al. (2005). Five was chosen as an upper bound because it is the highest possible base uncertainty value defined by Frischknecht et al. (2005). Neither uncertainty due to characterization models, nor due to meth- odological choices were assessed.
28: 2.2.1.1. Objectives. The goal of this study was to perform an ISO 14044- compliant (ISO, 2006a) cradle-to-farm gate attributional environmental LCA of the Canadian egg industry, differentiated by housing system. Previously, Pelletier (2017) performed a similar assessment using cradle-to-customer system boundaries, and data collected representing 2012 flocks. The current study updates this baseline, national-level LCA using data collected during 2017, and Summer and Fall 2019, and compares the life cycle impacts of Canadian egg production systems to

2.2.1.2. System boundaries and functional unit. The system boundaries for this study include all relevant material, energy, and emission flows linked to all processes in the cradle-to-farm gate egg supply chain in Canada (Fig. 1). These include breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, and layer facilities. Only egg production facilities and management of manure produced during egg production were included in the foreground pro- cesses. All other processes were considered part of the background system and were based on the 2012 Canadian baseline model (Pelletier, 2017). Transportation between processes is also considered. The func- tional unit is one tonne of eggs at the farm gate. The functional unit and system boundaries have been chosen in line with those used in Pelletier (2017) to ensure comparisons between results can be made on a meth- odologically consistent basis.

2.2.1.3. Co-product allocation. Allocation between coproducts (i.e. eggs and spent hens at the end of the egg production process) was performed on the basis of gross chemical energy content. This was chosen as an appropriate physical allocation criteria as it more accurately reflects the proportions of biological energy dedicated to production of eggs and meat by hens. A detailed discussion of this principle is available in Pelletier and Tyedmers (2011) and Pelletier et al. (2015). Coproducts were defined as those products that are used in some further economic activity. Spent hens used for human consumption or rendering for ani- mal feeds were allocated to, while those buried, composted, incinerated, or otherwise disposed of as waste were not. Allocation factors were calculated based on an assumed caloric density of 1520 kcal/kg for eggs (Egg Farmers of Canada, 2018), and 1906.43 kcal/kg for spent hens (Pelletier, 2017). Default background allocation factors were used on a mass basis.

2.2.1.4. Cut-off criteria and exclusions. Inputs and emissions associated with production and maintenance of infrastructure were excluded as they generally make small contributions to total environmental impacts (i.e. <5%), which decrease further when amortized against total pro- duction over their life-span (roughly 30 years for a layer barn) (Li, 2020). Feed inputs contributing less than 0.1% of the national, production-weighted average feed composition for layers feeds were excluded. A similar cut off threshold was used when modelling feed formulations for breeder and pullet flocks, unless the feed input had already been modelled as part of the layer feed formulation. Antibiotics, cleaners, and poultry enteric fermentation were also excluded in calculation of life cycle emissions due to their negligible relevance.
Land use changes, tillage regimes, and soil amendments related to

production of agricultural feed stocks and livestock for use in feed for- mulations may have large impacts on soil organic carbon and CO2 sequestration (Levasseur et al., 2012; Regina and Alakukku, 2010; Xu et al., 2019). However, these impacts were also excluded due to high levels of uncertainty in available modeling methodologies regarding rates of carbon sequestration/loss, and longevity of sequestration effects (Goglio et al., 2015). In addition, irrigation water was excluded as previous analyses of systems producing feed inputs included in this study suggest very limited irrigation inputs (AFSC, 2020; Pelletier, 2017; StatsCan, 2013).

2.2.2.6.0. Egg production data. Foreground data for egg production was collected via survey as previously described. Separate production weighted averages were calculated for each non-energy input based on a four-step process. All eggs across each housing system were assumed to have an average mass of 60g. This mass represents a midpoint between "large" and "extra large" sized eggs, rounded up, as defined by the Ca- nadian Food Inspection Agency (Canadian Food Inspection Agency, 2021).

Since all calculations were production weighted, all those survey responses which did not provide information on production were removed. Next, all responses were scaled to a one-year long (i.e. 365 day) laying cycle in order to account for variability in cycle length be- tween flocks (average length: 365.4 I 14.11 days; maximum: 434 days; minimum: 308 days). After scaling, production outliers were informally identified in accordance with Laurikkala et al. (2000). Using this method, a data point was identified as an outlier if the value was greater than quartile 3 + 1.5 X interquartile range, or less than quartile 1 1.5 X interquartile range. Dozens of eggs produced per hen per year was chosen as the metric for outlier detection to account for variability in production due to differences in flock sizes between surveys. Production outliers were detected individually for different housing systems to ac- count for variability in production across systems. If a survey response was found to be a production outlier it was removed completely. Following removal of production outliers, a second stage of outlier identification was performed using the same method for each of the individual inputs. If a survey was found to be an outlier for a specific input, or it did not provide a response for that input, it was removed from calculation of the production weighted average. It is important to note that this did not preclude other values from that data set being used in calculation of other production weighted averages. Outlier detection and calculation of production weighted averages
Fertilizers, pesticides, energy carriers, packaging, transportation, etc.
Fig. 1. System boundaries for the cradle-to-farm gate environmental LCA of the Canadian egg supply chain.

for energy inputs was difficult due to low response rates and large variability in reported energy consumption across farms. Energy con- sumption data was collected for electricity, diesel, gasoline, liquified petroleum gas/propane, fuel oil, and natural gas. Across every barn surveyed, electricity was the only consistently used type of energy for lighting, ventilation, and other automated systems. Since electricity is consistently used to some degree in all barns, data sets for which elec- tricity consumption data were not provided were removed in calculation of production weighted electricity consumption. In some cases, farmers reported electricity consumption in terms of the amount of money spent during that production period. When this was the case, consumption was calculated based on average electricity prices in each province as re- ported by Urban (2020).

Outliers for electricity consumption were identified through com- parison of calculated values with the ranges reported by Pelletier (2017, supplementary tables S16-20). If a value fell outside of the previously reported range, it was identified as an outlier and removed. This method of outlier detection was not applied to electricity consumption for either single- or multi-tier free run barns because the range previously reported by Pelletier (2017, supplementary table S18) did not differentiate be- tween different free run systems, and because the reported range was much narrower than that reported for any other system. As such, only obvious outliers identified via visual inspection were removed to pre- serve the variability of the data sets.

In contrast to electricity, other forms of energy are variously used across farms for processes such as heating, running backup generators, barn cleaning, etc. Since the other sources of energy covered by the survey are not consistently applied across all farms, if a farm did not report consumption levels for a particular fuel it was assumed to be zero. These zeroes were subsequently included in calculation of production weighted average consumptions. Outlier detection could not be per- formed on these data due to the inconsistent use of these energy sources, the inability to differentiate between zero and missing values, and the large variation seen in reported energy consumption levels across those farms that did report consumption. As such, only obvious outliers on the very high end of the spectrum that could be identified visually were removed.

In some cases, insufficient data was collected for calculation of production weighted averages for non-electricity energy sources. When this was the case, the average values presented in Pelletier (2017, sup- plementary tables S15-20) were used. Specifically, the following values reported by Pelletier (2017) were used: all non-electricity energy inputs for single-tier free run production; and all non-electricity energy inputs for multi-tier free run, free range, and organic production with the exception of diesel. No updated data were calculated for transportation values. As such, these values are the same as those reported by Pelletier (2017). The values reported for free run production were applied to both single- and multi-tier free run models. Conversion factors used for calculating energy consumption where necessary are presented in sup- plementary table S1. Provincial electricity models as well as models for all other energy inputs were taken from the ecoInvent database, (Frischknecht al., 2005).

2.2.2.7.0. Modeling N, P, and methane emissions from poultry man- ure. Actual manure removal amount data were not collected, as previ- ous efforts to collect estimates of manure volumes from farmers resulted in a data set of low resolution and quality (Pelletier 2017). A standard manure excretion rate was assumed, scaled relative to the feed conver- sion efficiencies calculated for each housing system (Pelletier, 2017; Pelletier et al., 2014). Importantly, this excretion rate did not take into account the impact of hen genetics, an important factor in determining feed conversion efficiency (Yuan et al., 2017, 2015). Nitrogen and phosphorous excretion rates were calculated based on a nutrient mass balance model using feed composition and assuming that 2.2% of hen body mass is N and 0.6% is P, and that eggs contain 1.7% N and 0.21% P in accordance with Koelsch (2007). From the estimated P excretion, P emissions were calculated assuming a 2.9% run-off rate at time of

application of manure to agricultural lands, in line with Dalgaard et al. (2008). Estimates of N excretion were subsequently used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia, and nitric oxide emissions from manure management, and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions in accordance with IPCC (2006) Tier 2 pro- tocols. Default emission factors for emissions at time of deposition, storage, and application were used. Calculation of nitrogenous emis- sions accounted for the proportions of different manure management systems used across each housing system (i.e. covered/uncovered dry and liquid storage, and direct field application without storage) through the calculation of production weighted average emission factors. A ni- trate leaching rate of 20% was assumed for calculation of N losses due to leaching during manure storage for uncovered manure storage, and 0% for covered manure storage. Methane emissions from manure manage- ment were calculated in accordance with IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols, taking into account the proportions and residency time of manure in different storage systems. Methane emissions factors for layers were 0.03 and 1.2 kg CH4 /head/year for hens in dry and wet manure man- agement systems respectively. Field level methane emissions resulting from direct application of manure to agricultural fields were not included, as no such corresponding emissions factors could be found in the IPCC (2006) methods.

2.2.2.8.0. Modeling feed input supply chains. Background data for feed supply chains was collected from several secondary sources. Energy inputs for feed milling processes were derived from LCA studies of the US egg industry (Pelletier, Ibarburu, & Xin, 2013; Pelletier et al., 2014), as in Pelletier (2017). Feed formulations for breeder flock, pullet, and layer facilities are representative of production-weighted average com- positions based on producer-reported data collected by Pelletier (2017). These formulations, in turn, dictated, material inputs to feed milling processes. Canadian-average models were used for production of wheat, corn, and soy, adjusted to represent appropriate input levels of fertilizer and pesticides, seed, and energy inputs, as well as yields. Models for barley production were developed based on input data per tonne taken from (S&T)2 (2008), adjusted to reflect 2012 Canadian average yields of 2.9 tonnes/ha as reported by StatsCan. Models for canola production were developed based on input data per tonne from Cheminfo and (S&T)2 (2013), adjusted to reflect 2012 Canadian average yields of 1.6 tonnes/ha as reported by StatsCan. Inventory data representing organic corn, wheat, soy, and canola production for use in organic feed milling processes were taken from Pelletier et al. (2008a). Field level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitrate, and carbon dioxide emissions associated with field crop production were calculated according to IPCC Tier 1 protocols (2006) using default emission factors. Phosphate emissions from run-off were assumed to be 2.9% following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizer and pesticide inputs were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, while seed inputs were assumed to be transported 100 km by truck. Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were used, with inventory data models for individual fertilizer inputs taken from the EcoInvent database (Frischknecht et al., 2005). These models are representative of average production conditions across Europe.

In addition, peas also make significant contributions to the modelled feed formulations. Models representing the production of peas were the only feed crop production model that differed from those used in Pel- letier (2017). Rather, a prairie (i.e. Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Man- itoba) average pea production model was used (Dutta et al., 2020). This model was developed using production-weighted averages based on approximately 300 surveys of Canadian pea farmers across the prairie provinces, which collected data on field level inputs, activities, and production. Field level emissions associated with pea production were modelled using the process-based DNDC model, adjusted for Canadian feed crop production (Li et al., 1992).

Many livestock derived feed ingredients were also included in the modeled feed formulations, including fat and meat/bone meal products rendered from cattle, swine, and poultry processing co-products, as well as poultry feather meal. Models representing beef and swine production

were adapted from models of US production systems (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with changes made to represent Canadian production conditions. Models for ruminant and porcine products are derived from Pelletier et al. (2014). Inventory models for other inputs to feed pro- duction including sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids, and other pre-mixes and supplements were derived from the EcoInvent database (2005), with all mineral inputs modeled as salt. The amino acid DL-methionine was used as a proxy for all amino acid inputs, and was modeled using inventory data as reported by Binder (2003). Other pre-mixes and supplements were excluded from feed models due to their wide variability in composition, and the lack of detailed composition data.

2.2.2.9.0. Data quality assessment. In accordance with ISO 14044 (2006b), data quality was assessed for foreground (i.e. egg production and layer manure management) processes, as well as background pro- cesses from Pelletier (2017). Background processes for which data quality was assessed include pullet, hatchery, and breeder flocks and associated manure management activities, feed milling processes for layer, pullet, and breeder flocks, and feed crop production models (Fig. 1). Data quality was assessed for each flow into and out of these processes using the default EcoInvent pedigree matrix, as described by Table 1 in Ciroth et al. (2016). Specifically, data quality was assessed in regards to reliability, completeness, and temporal, geographical, and technological correlation. Importantly, data quality assessment of this nature includes a degree of subjectivity. For each flow assessed, data quality scores were defined in relation to how well the data represented by the flow actually represented the modeling goal for each flow. Data quality was not assessed for background processes from LCI databases.

2.2.2.10.0. Data uncertainty assessment. Based on recommended best practices for uncertainty assessment in LCA (Bamber et al., 2019), parameter uncertainty (i.e. uncertainty in inventory data) was propa- gated using Monte Carlo simulations with 1000 runs. Where feasible, uncertainty factors for egg production were based on a combination of calculated geometric standard deviations, and uncertainty contributions from the pedigree matrix entries, as defined in Ciroth et al. (2016). Null values were not taken into account during calculation of geometric standard deviations for energy flows. In some cases, small sample sizes resulted in calculation of uncertainty factors several times higher than what would be expected for the agricultural sector, as noted by Frisch- knecht et al. (2005). Because of this, calculated uncertainty values greater than 5 were removed and replaced with the corresponding generic, sector-specific uncertainty value from Frischknecht et al. (2005). Five was chosen as an upper bound because it is the highest possible base uncertainty value defined by Frischknecht et al. (2005). Neither uncertainty due to characterization models, nor due to meth- odological choices were assessed.

Specifically, data quality was assessed in regards to reliability, completeness, and temporal, geographical, and technological correlation. Importantly, data quality assessment of this nature includes a degree of subjectivity. For each flow assessed, data quality scores were defined in relation to how well the data represented by the flow actually represented the modeling goal for each flow. Data quality was not assessed for background processes from LCI databases.

2.2.2.10.0. Data uncertainty assessment. Based on recommended best practices for uncertainty assessment in LCA (Bamber et al., 2019), parameter uncertainty (i.e. uncertainty in inventory data) was propa- gated using Monte Carlo simulations with 1000 runs. Where feasible, uncertainty factors for egg production were based on a combination of calculated geometric standard deviations, and uncertainty contributions from the pedigree matrix entries, as defined in Ciroth et al. (2016). Null values were not taken into account during calculation of geometric standard deviations for energy flows. In some cases, small sample sizes resulted in calculation of uncertainty factors several times higher than what would be expected for the agricultural sector, as noted by Frisch- knecht et al. (2005). Because of this, calculated uncertainty values greater than 5 were removed and replaced with the corresponding generic, sector-specific uncertainty value from Frischknecht et al. (2005). Five was chosen as an upper bound because it is the highest possible base uncertainty value defined by Frischknecht et al. (2005). Neither uncertainty due to characterization models, nor due to meth- odological choices were assessed.
30: Ozone layer depletion, human toxicity, abiotic depletion, photo- chemical oxidation, acidification, eutrophication, terrestrial, and fresh

and marine aquatic ecotoxicity, and global warming potential were assessed using the CML 2 Baseline 2000 impact assessment method (Guinée et al., 2002). Global warming potential was calculated over a 100-year time horizon. Direct land use was not evaluated due to insuf- ficient data. As in Pelletier (2017), withdrawals of salt water were excluded form the analysis, as was water flowing through turbines for electricity generation. Impact category results were calculated using problem-oriented mid-point indicators, rather than damage-level end-point indicators. All calculations were performed using OpenLCA version 1.10.2 (GreenDelta, 2020). Comparisons were made to previ- ously reported results (Pelletier, 2017). However, because uncertainty was not calculated as part of the analysis performed by Pelletier (2017), statistical analyses between the results of the two models were not possible.
31: In total, 206 surveys were received during the Summer and Fall 2019 data collection periods from which data could be extracted. Of these surveys, 73.3% (151/206) were fully completed, while the remainder were partially completed. The largest number of responses was received for conventional systems (46.12%), while the fewest responses came from organic systems (5.83%). Representativeness of the collected sample ranged from 0% to 50% for individual housing systems within provinces. Nationally, this sample represented 12.5% of conventional production, 24.87% of enriched production, 25.77% of cage-free (i.e. single- and multi-tier free run and free range), and 15.38% of organic production (Table 1). Representativeness of the total number of farms throughout provinces and nationally range from 0% to 44% (Table 1). The collected data was supplemented with additional data sets collected during 2017 (Table S2).
32: With the exception of those inputs which remained constant between the models developed here and those reported in Pelletier (2017), input and output levels generally decreased across all housing systems with few exceptions (Table 2). Notable gains were made in feed conversion efficiency, reflected by lower feed inputs, in all housing systems except for free range which exhibited much higher levels of feed use than previously reported. Pullet inputs (i.e. number of ~20 week old laying hens placed in the barn at the beginning of the lay cycle) per tonne of eggs produced decreased across all housing systems. Mortality rates were lower in conventional, single- and multi-tier free run, and organic production systems, and higher than previously reported in enriched and free range systems. Notable differences were seen in the number of weeks manure spent in barns across alternative systems. The number of weeks manure was left in barns decreased by up to 20 weeks across all alternative systems except for single-tier free run systems which saw an increase of roughly 51 weeks. Differences were also observed in the number of weeks manure spent in storage. Increases in manure storage time of between roughly 3 and 11 weeks were reported in conventional, single-a multi-tier free run, and free range systems, while enriched and organic systems reported decreases of roughly 7-9 weeks. manure and mass of mortalities and spent hens, result from the observed decreases in associated input levels (i.e. increased efficiency with respect to feed and pullet inputs). Notable differences were seen in the number of weeks manure spent in barns across alternative systems. The number of weeks manure was left in barns decreased by up to 20 weeks across all alternative systems except for single-tier free run systems which saw an increase of roughly 51 weeks. Differences were also observed in the number of weeks manure spent in storage. Increases in manure storage time of between roughly 3 and 11 weeks were reported in conventional, single-a multi-tier free run, and free range systems, while enriched and organic systems reported decreases of roughly 7-9 weeks. Life cycle inventory data for feed milling, feed crop produc- tion, pullet, hatchery, and breeder facilities may be found in the sup- plementary information file of Pelletier (2017). Complete ranges for each inventory value may be found in supplementary information tables S3-S8.

manure and mass of mortalities and spent hens, result from the observed decreases in associated input levels (i.e. increased efficiency with respect to feed and pullet inputs). Notable differences were seen in the number of weeks manure spent in barns across alternative systems. The number of weeks manure was left in barns decreased by up to 20 weeks across all alternative systems except for single-tier free run systems which saw an increase of roughly 51 weeks. Differences were also observed in the number of weeks manure spent in storage. Increases in manure storage time of between roughly 3 and 11 weeks were reported in conventional, single-a multi-tier free run, and free range systems, while enriched and organic systems reported decreases of roughly 7-9 weeks. Life cycle inventory data for feed milling, feed crop produc- tion, pullet, hatchery, and breeder facilities may be found in the sup- plementary information file of Pelletier (2017). Complete ranges for each inventory value may be found in supplementary information tables S3-S8.

Blank spaces indicate inventory data that was not included in the model for that particular housing system. For complete ranges, see supplementary tables S2-7.

manure and mass of mortalities and spent hens, result from the observed decreases in associated input levels (i.e. increased efficiency with respect to feed and pullet inputs). Notable differences were seen in the number of weeks manure spent in barns across alternative systems. The number of weeks manure was left in barns decreased by up to 20 weeks across all alternative systems except for single-tier free run systems which saw an increase of roughly 51 weeks. Differences were also observed in the number of weeks manure spent in storage. Increases in manure storage time of between roughly 3 and 11 weeks were reported in conventional, single-a multi-tier free run, and free range systems, while enriched and organic systems reported decreases of roughly 7-9 weeks. Life cycle inventory data for feed milling, feed crop produc- tion, pullet, hatchery, and breeder facilities may be found in the sup- plementary information file of Pelletier (2017). Complete ranges for each inventory value may be found in supplementary information tables S3-S8.

manure and mass of mortalities and spent hens, result from the observed decreases in associated input levels (i.e. increased efficiency with respect to feed and pullet inputs). Notable differences were seen in the number of weeks manure spent in barns across alternative systems. The number of weeks manure was left in barns decreased by up to 20 weeks across all alternative systems except for single-tier free run systems which saw an increase of roughly 51 weeks. Differences were also observed in the number of weeks manure spent in storage. Increases in manure storage time of between roughly 3 and 11 weeks were reported in conventional, single-a multi-tier free run, and free range systems, while enriched and organic systems reported decreases of roughly 7-9 weeks. Life cycle inventory data for feed milling, feed crop produc- tion, pullet, hatchery, and breeder facilities may be found in the sup- plementary information file of Pelletier (2017). Complete ranges for each inventory value may be found in supplementary information tables S3-S8.
48: The entire life cycle inventory model (with proprietary, third party LCI providers disconnected) used to generate the LCIA results may be found on the Open Science Framework at https://osf.io/4qetu/?sho w=view. Fig.s 2-4 present the cradle to egg farm gate life cycle impact assessment results for production of one tonne of eggs in each housing system for the impact categories acidifying, eutrophying, and GHG emissions, alongside the corresponding values previously reported by Pelletier (2017). These Fig.s also present the contributions to total im- pacts made by different groups of inputs, namely pullets, feed, water, energy, manure management, and transportation. Exact values can be found in supplementary information table S8. Results for additional impact categories not previously reported by Pelletier (2017) are pre- sented in supplementary table S9.

Notable differences can be seen between the results presented by Pelletier (2017), and those presented here. Acidifying emissions decreased for conventional, enriched, single-tier free run, and organic production systems by between 0.5%-4.78%, and increased for multi-tier free run and free range production systems. While multi-tier production systems only saw roughly 1% increases in acidifying

emissions, these emissions increased by roughly 21% for free range systems. Free range systems also exhibited large increases in GHG and eutrophying emissions (21.42% and 31.97%, respectively). GHG emis- sions decreased in conventional, free run, and organic production sys- tems by between 0.1%-4.88%, and increased in enriched systems by 1.07%. Eutrophying emissions increased in all housing systems by 3.32%-31.97%, with the exception of organic systems which exhibited a 0.16% decrease. Importantly, the increase in acidifying emissions observed in multi-tier free run production may be a result of sampling bias in Pelletier (2017), as the previous analysis did not differentiate between single- and multi-tier free run production.

Across all three of these impact categories, and most others, feed inputs were the greatest contributor. Decreases in feed use of roughly 3.5%-10% were observed compared to Pelletier (2017) for all housing systems except free range and organic. These changes in feed use effi- ciency are key drivers of the differences in results presented here, and by Pelletier (2017), particularly for free range systems in which feed use increased considerably. Interestingly, while feed use also increased in organic systems, they were the only system for which all three of these impacts decreased. The emissions decreases observed in organic systems are therefore likely due to a combination of factors, including decreases in pullet inputs, and differences in manure management practices be- tween the samples outweighing the contributions from increased feed use. This explanation is supported by the fact that, relative to non-organic systems, feed inputs in organic systems make comparatively smaller contributions to cumulative environmental impacts. This is driven by differences in feed formulations between organic and non-organic production systems, particularly the exclusion of animal products in organic feed, the upstream impacts of which may be very large (Beauchemin et al., 2011; McGeough et al., 2012). Further, organic field crop production is also associated with lower GHG emissions than their conventional counterparts due to large differences in allowable fertilizer inputs - in particular, the reliance on biological nitrogen fix- ation in place of synthetic nitrogen fertilizers (Pelletier et al., 2008b).
Many of the decreases in impacts observed may be attributed to decreases in feed use, which provide a double-benefit by also decreasing amounts of manure excreted and emissions related to manure

Error bars are only presented for impacts calculated using updated data, and are representative of standard error.

Fig. 3. Eutrophying emissions (kg PO - eq.) associated with the production of one tonne of eggs in Canada across different housing systems using updated data collected in 2019, and associated values previously reported by Pelletier (2017) based on 2012 data. Error bars are only presented for impacts calculated using updated data, and are representative of standard error.

Fig. 4. GHG emissions (kg CO2 eq.) associated with the production of one tonne of eggs in Canada across different housing systems using updated data collected in 2019, and associated values previously reported by Pelletier (2017) based on 2012 data. Error bars are only presented for impacts calculated using updated data, and are representative of standard error.

management, another key driver of many environmental impacts. De- creases in impacts are also driven by decreases in pullet inputs. Inter- estingly, there were some cases in which feed and/or pullet inputs decreased compared to previous levels, but increases in impacts were still observed (i.e. eutrophying emissions in non-organic systems, and acidifying emissions in multi-tier free run production). The observed increases in acidifying emissions in multi-tier free run production may be a result of sampling bias, as Pelletier (2017) did not previously differentiate between single- and multi-tier free run housing. The observed increases in eutrophying emissions are likely due to changes in manure management practices within the sample. Finally, contributions from energy inputs also decreased across all three of these impact cat- egories and housing systems while contributions from water and trans- portation inputs remained comparable.

Of the additional impact categories investigated here not included in Pelletier (2017), emissions related to feed, pullets, and manure man- agement continue to be the main contributors to most, while contribu- tions from energy and water inputs, and transportation remain fairly small. The exception to this are the freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity and marine aquatic ecotoxicity impact categories, for which energy inputs replace manure management as a key contributor. Pullet inputs make

large contributions to many impacts for the same reasons as those in layer systems - inputs of feed, manure management practices, and up- stream emissions associated with breeder flocks. Contributions from pullet inputs are particularly noticeable in abiotic depletion for organic systems. This impact category is the only one in which organic systems do not have the lowest impacts; rather, organic systems have the highest abiotic depletion impacts of all systems. This is due to the large amounts of natural gas used in organic pullet production systems (Pelletier 2017, supplementary table S15), production of which makes large contribu- tions to abiotic depletion.

Overall, uncertainty was quite low for GHG, acidifying, and eutro- phying emissions, ozone layer, and abiotic depletion, photochemical oxidation, and terrestrial ecotoxicity across all housing systems. In contrast, uncertainty was quite high for human toxicity, and freshwater and marine aquatic ecotoxicity, reflected by relatively higher standard errors associated with those impact category results. Notably, impact categories found to have higher uncertainty in results are also those for which energy inputs made relatively large contributions to total life cycle environmental impacts, as opposed to the majority of contribu- tions coming from feed and manure management processes. This in- dicates the relatively high uncertainty in these impact categories may be

related to upstream energy production processes. It may also be related to the relatively high variability seen in energy inputs across all housing systems, resulting in the use of high uncertainty factors, compared to the lower uncertainty values associated with other farm level inputs and processes.

Importantly, the results presented here demonstrate the presence of trade-offs in environmental impacts between different housing systems. When ranking systems based on their environmental performance, organic production consistently has the lowest impacts for all impact categories investigated, except abiotic depletion, for which its impacts are the highest (Fig. 5). Free range production consistently had the highest impacts of all systems, except for in abiotic depletion. Multi-tier free run consistently ranked fourth or fifth for all impacts, except for ozone layer depletion in which it was tied for third. The final notable trade-off is in enriched production, which consistently ranks behind conventional cages in all impact categories except photochemical oxidation, and GHG emissions. This suggests that a transition from conventional to enriched systems may be beneficial for the carbon footprint of the Canadian egg industry, but detrimental to many other environmental sustainability impacts.

related to upstream energy production processes. It may also be related to the relatively high variability seen in energy inputs across all housing systems, resulting in the use of high uncertainty factors, compared to the lower uncertainty values associated with other farm level inputs and processes.

Importantly, the results presented here demonstrate the presence of trade-offs in environmental impacts between different housing systems. When ranking systems based on their environmental performance, organic production consistently has the lowest impacts for all impact categories investigated, except abiotic depletion, for which its impacts are the highest (Fig. 5). Free range production consistently had the highest impacts of all systems, except for in abiotic depletion. Multi-tier free run consistently ranked fourth or fifth for all impacts, except for ozone layer depletion in which it was tied for third. The final notable trade-off is in enriched production, which consistently ranks behind conventional cages in all impact categories except photochemical oxidation, and GHG emissions. This suggests that a transition from conventional to enriched systems may be beneficial for the carbon footprint of the Canadian egg industry, but detrimental to many other environmental sustainability impacts.
51: The entire life cycle inventory model (with proprietary, third party LCI providers disconnected) used to generate the LCIA results may be found on the Open Science Framework at https://osf.io/4qetu/?sho w=view. Fig.s 2-4 present the cradle to egg farm gate life cycle impact assessment results for production of one tonne of eggs in each housing system for the impact categories acidifying, eutrophying, and GHG emissions, alongside the corresponding values previously reported by Pelletier (2017). These Fig.s also present the contributions to total im- pacts made by different groups of inputs, namely pullets, feed, water, energy, manure management, and transportation. Exact values can be found in supplementary information table S8. Results for additional impact categories not previously reported by Pelletier (2017) are pre- sented in supplementary table S9.

Notable differences can be seen between the results presented by Pelletier (2017), and those presented here. Acidifying emissions decreased for conventional, enriched, single-tier free run, and organic production systems by between 0.5%-4.78%, and increased for multi-tier free run and free range production systems. While multi-tier production systems only saw roughly 1% increases in acidifying

emissions, these emissions increased by roughly 21% for free range systems. Free range systems also exhibited large increases in GHG and eutrophying emissions (21.42% and 31.97%, respectively). GHG emis- sions decreased in conventional, free run, and organic production sys- tems by between 0.1%-4.88%, and increased in enriched systems by 1.07%. Eutrophying emissions increased in all housing systems by 3.32%-31.97%, with the exception of organic systems which exhibited a 0.16% decrease. Importantly, the increase in acidifying emissions observed in multi-tier free run production may be a result of sampling bias in Pelletier (2017), as the previous analysis did not differentiate between single- and multi-tier free run production.

Across all three of these impact categories, and most others, feed inputs were the greatest contributor. Decreases in feed use of roughly 3.5%-10% were observed compared to Pelletier (2017) for all housing systems except free range and organic. These changes in feed use effi- ciency are key drivers of the differences in results presented here, and by Pelletier (2017), particularly for free range systems in which feed use increased considerably. Interestingly, while feed use also increased in organic systems, they were the only system for which all three of these impacts decreased. The emissions decreases observed in organic systems are therefore likely due to a combination of factors, including decreases in pullet inputs, and differences in manure management practices be- tween the samples outweighing the contributions from increased feed use. This explanation is supported by the fact that, relative to non-organic systems, feed inputs in organic systems make comparatively smaller contributions to cumulative environmental impacts. This is driven by differences in feed formulations between organic and non-organic production systems, particularly the exclusion of animal products in organic feed, the upstream impacts of which may be very large (Beauchemin et al., 2011; McGeough et al., 2012). Further, organic field crop production is also associated with lower GHG emissions than their conventional counterparts due to large differences in allowable fertilizer inputs - in particular, the reliance on biological nitrogen fix- ation in place of synthetic nitrogen fertilizers (Pelletier et al., 2008b).
Many of the decreases in impacts observed may be attributed to decreases in feed use, which provide a double-benefit by also decreasing amounts of manure excreted and emissions related to manure

management, another key driver of many environmental impacts. De- creases in impacts are also driven by decreases in pullet inputs. Inter- estingly, there were some cases in which feed and/or pullet inputs decreased compared to previous levels, but increases in impacts were still observed (i.e. eutrophying emissions in non-organic systems, and acidifying emissions in multi-tier free run production). The observed increases in acidifying emissions in multi-tier free run production may be a result of sampling bias, as Pelletier (2017) did not previously differentiate between single- and multi-tier free run housing. The observed increases in eutrophying emissions are likely due to changes in manure management practices within the sample. Finally, contributions from energy inputs also decreased across all three of these impact cat- egories and housing systems while contributions from water and trans- portation inputs remained comparable.

Of the additional impact categories investigated here not included in Pelletier (2017), emissions related to feed, pullets, and manure man- agement continue to be the main contributors to most, while contribu- tions from energy and water inputs, and transportation remain fairly small. The exception to this are the freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity and marine aquatic ecotoxicity impact categories, for which energy inputs replace manure management as a key contributor. Pullet inputs make

large contributions to many impacts for the same reasons as those in layer systems - inputs of feed, manure management practices, and up- stream emissions associated with breeder flocks. Contributions from pullet inputs are particularly noticeable in abiotic depletion for organic systems. This impact category is the only one in which organic systems do not have the lowest impacts; rather, organic systems have the highest abiotic depletion impacts of all systems. This is due to the large amounts of natural gas used in organic pullet production systems (Pelletier 2017, supplementary table S15), production of which makes large contribu- tions to abiotic depletion.

Overall, uncertainty was quite low for GHG, acidifying, and eutro- phying emissions, ozone layer, and abiotic depletion, photochemical oxidation, and terrestrial ecotoxicity across all housing systems. In contrast, uncertainty was quite high for human toxicity, and freshwater and marine aquatic ecotoxicity, reflected by relatively higher standard errors associated with those impact category results. This in- dicates the relatively high uncertainty in these impact categories may be

related to upstream energy production processes. GHG emis- sions decreased in conventional, free run, and organic production sys- tems by between 0.1%-4.88%, and increased in enriched systems by 1.07%. Eutrophying emissions increased in all housing systems by 3.32%-31.97%, with the exception of organic systems which exhibited a 0.16% decrease. Importantly, the increase in acidifying emissions observed in multi-tier free run production may be a result of sampling bias in Pelletier (2017), as the previous analysis did not differentiate between single- and multi-tier free run production.

Across all three of these impact categories, and most others, feed inputs were the greatest contributor. Decreases in feed use of roughly 3.5%-10% were observed compared to Pelletier (2017) for all housing systems except free range and organic. These changes in feed use effi- ciency are key drivers of the differences in results presented here, and by Pelletier (2017), particularly for free range systems in which feed use increased considerably. Interestingly, while feed use also increased in organic systems, they were the only system for which all three of these impacts decreased. The emissions decreases observed in organic systems are therefore likely due to a combination of factors, including decreases in pullet inputs, and differences in manure management practices be- tween the samples outweighing the contributions from increased feed use. This explanation is supported by the fact that, relative to non-organic systems, feed inputs in organic systems make comparatively smaller contributions to cumulative environmental impacts. This is driven by differences in feed formulations between organic and non-organic production systems, particularly the exclusion of animal products in organic feed, the upstream impacts of which may be very large (Beauchemin et al., 2011; McGeough et al., 2012). Further, organic field crop production is also associated with lower GHG emissions than their conventional counterparts due to large differences in allowable fertilizer inputs - in particular, the reliance on biological nitrogen fix- ation in place of synthetic nitrogen fertilizers (Pelletier et al., 2008b).
Many of the decreases in impacts observed may be attributed to decreases in feed use, which provide a double-benefit by also decreasing amounts of manure excreted and emissions related to manure

Error bars are only presented for impacts calculated using updated data, and are representative of standard error.

Fig. 3. Eutrophying emissions (kg PO - eq.) associated with the production of one tonne of eggs in Canada across different housing systems using updated data collected in 2019, and associated values previously reported by Pelletier (2017) based on 2012 data. GHG emissions (kg CO2 eq.) associated with the production of one tonne of eggs in Canada across different housing systems using updated data collected in 2019, and associated values previously reported by Pelletier (2017) based on 2012 data. management, another key driver of many environmental impacts. De- creases in impacts are also driven by decreases in pullet inputs. Inter- estingly, there were some cases in which feed and/or pullet inputs decreased compared to previous levels, but increases in impacts were still observed (i.e. eutrophying emissions in non-organic systems, and acidifying emissions in multi-tier free run production). The observed increases in acidifying emissions in multi-tier free run production may be a result of sampling bias, as Pelletier (2017) did not previously differentiate between single- and multi-tier free run housing. The observed increases in eutrophying emissions are likely due to changes in manure management practices within the sample. Finally, contributions from energy inputs also decreased across all three of these impact cat- egories and housing systems while contributions from water and trans- portation inputs remained comparable.

Of the additional impact categories investigated here not included in Pelletier (2017), emissions related to feed, pullets, and manure man- agement continue to be the main contributors to most, while contribu- tions from energy and water inputs, and transportation remain fairly small. The exception to this are the freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity and marine aquatic ecotoxicity impact categories, for which energy inputs replace manure management as a key contributor. Pullet inputs make

large contributions to many impacts for the same reasons as those in layer systems - inputs of feed, manure management practices, and up- stream emissions associated with breeder flocks. Contributions from pullet inputs are particularly noticeable in abiotic depletion for organic systems. This impact category is the only one in which organic systems do not have the lowest impacts; rather, organic systems have the highest abiotic depletion impacts of all systems. This is due to the large amounts of natural gas used in organic pullet production systems (Pelletier 2017, supplementary table S15), production of which makes large contribu- tions to abiotic depletion.

Overall, uncertainty was quite low for GHG, acidifying, and eutro- phying emissions, ozone layer, and abiotic depletion, photochemical oxidation, and terrestrial ecotoxicity across all housing systems. In contrast, uncertainty was quite high for human toxicity, and freshwater and marine aquatic ecotoxicity, reflected by relatively higher standard errors associated with those impact category results. Notably, impact categories found to have higher uncertainty in results are also those for which energy inputs made relatively large contributions to total life cycle environmental impacts, as opposed to the majority of contribu- tions coming from feed and manure management processes. This in- dicates the relatively high uncertainty in these impact categories may be

related to upstream energy production processes. It may also be related to the relatively high variability seen in energy inputs across all housing systems, resulting in the use of high uncertainty factors, compared to the lower uncertainty values associated with other farm level inputs and processes.

Importantly, the results presented here demonstrate the presence of trade-offs in environmental impacts between different housing systems. When ranking systems based on their environmental performance, organic production consistently has the lowest impacts for all impact categories investigated, except abiotic depletion, for which its impacts are the highest (Fig. 5). Free range production consistently had the highest impacts of all systems, except for in abiotic depletion. Multi-tier free run consistently ranked fourth or fifth for all impacts, except for ozone layer depletion in which it was tied for third. The final notable trade-off is in enriched production, which consistently ranks behind conventional cages in all impact categories except photochemical oxidation, and GHG emissions. This suggests that a transition from conventional to enriched systems may be beneficial for the carbon footprint of the Canadian egg industry, but detrimental to many other environmental sustainability impacts.
56: Compared to the results previously presented by Pelletier (2017), the LCA models developed here demonstrate general reductions in input levels, and life cycle environmental impacts. The models developed using this updated data further build on Pelletier (2017) by introducing three key changes: the differentiation of free-run production systems into single- and multi-tier systems; the adoption of updated models for pea production used in feed production models; and the performance of quantitative uncertainty assessment. Additional environmental impacts not previously assessed are also included here.

The differentiation between single- and multi- tier free run systems in this analysis is a key development compared to Pelletier (2017). Free run production is currently the third most widespread production system in Canada, behind conventional and enriched cage production (Egg Farmers of Canada, 2020a). The proportion of farms employing free run systems is expected to grow as the industry transitions away from con- ventional cages. Importantly, the development of single- and multi-tier free run production system models as presented here will play an important role in informing egg farmers and policy makers of the environmental sustainability benefits and trade-offs associated with these systems. The results presented here, for example, indicate that

environmental performance in single-tier free run systems is the same or better than that in multi-tier systems for all impacts assessed, both of which outperformed in most cases by enriched production systems. This knowledge will aid farmers in making informed decisions as they continue to navigate the ongoing housing system transition taking place in Canada. Of note, however, this study did not take into account po- tential differences in land use between different systems. Lower acceptable stocking densities in cage-free systems necessitate greater farm-level land use by these systems, which may be exacerbated particularly in organic systems due to higher levels of land use in organic field crop production systems (Pelletier et al., 2008b). Differences in cumulative land use between housing systems should also be taken into account in sustainability decision making, as increases in organic egg production may have large-scale ripple effects in field-crop production systems around how land is allocated between conventional and organic field crops, and how crop products are allocated between human and animal consumption (Pelletier et al., 2008b). There may also be trade-offs to consider across other environmental impacts not assessed here.

Further, the use of updated models for pea production represents the first step in necessary updates to all of the feed input models presented in Pelletier (2017). Feed inputs make the greatest contribution to all of the impacts assessed, demonstrating the importance of robust, regionally specific feed crop production and feed formulation models in egg pro- duction LCAs. A recent review of Canadian agri-food LCI models indi- cated that current, Canadian-specific models for production of conventional and organic field crops, fertilizers, plant protection com- pounds, and other agricultural support activities are lacking (Turner et al., 2020). Such models must continue to be developed to decrease uncertainty associated with modelling of feed production. This would be of great benefit to all livestock industries in Canada, as feed inputs are consistently identified as key drivers of total life cycle environmental impacts across many livestock systems (Arsenault et al., 2009; Beau- chemin et al., 2011; McGeough et al., 2012; Pelletier, 2017). Improved modeling of feed production could also facilitate multi-objective opti- mization of feed formulations based on both least economic- and envi- ronmental cost, a strategy that could result in significant decreases in the environmental impacts of livestock systems (Heidari et al., 2021).

Further developments must also be made in modelling of pullet production, which makes large contributions to many of the environ- mental impacts assessed. Robust, Canadian specific data characterizing cage-free pullet production is lacking, and the caged-pullet production models used here were the same as those used in Pelletier (2017). While
Fig. 5. Relative ranking of environmental impacts per tonne of eggs produced in each housing system, across all impact categories.

pullet-related impacts have decreased compared to those presented in Pelletier (2017), the magnitude of the potential decreases may be underestimated given the absence of adequate data, particularly for cage-free pullet systems. Golden et al. (2012), for example, note that pullets reared in cage-free systems with range access consumed 0.79 kg/bird less feed than their cage-reared counterparts. The authors note that this is likely due to the replacement of feed with foraged materials, which allowed cage-free pullets to consume comparable amount of protein, despite consuming less formulated feed overall compared to caged pullets. The authors also note that there was no significant dif- ference in mortality rate between the two pullet systems (Golden et al., 2012). Similarly, differences in manure-management practices between caged and cage-free pullet production systems must also be considered, given the large contribution to environmental impacts made by manure-related emissions. Updating pullet production models to reflect differences between caged and cage-free systems is therefore imperative to the accuracy and robustness of LCA results for the Canadian egg industry.

Importantly, the results presented here indicate that conventional cages generally outperform non-organic alternatives in environmental impacts. It may seem therefore that the transition away from conven- tional cages may be a net negative for the environmental sustainability of Canadian egg production. What cannot be discounted, however, is the influence that producer experience has on determining eco-efficiency levels, in a phenomenon known as the "skipper effect" (González-García et al., 2015; Vázquez-Rowe and Tyedmers, 2013). Though the impact of producer experience on eco-efficiency levels has not be assessed in egg production systems, it is possible such a rela- tionship still exists. It is therefore likely that, as farmers continue to transition away from conventional cages and gain experience working in alternative systems, resource-use efficiency in these systems would in- crease, further decreasing environmental impacts. This possibility un- derscores the importance of continued monitoring of the environmental impacts of Canadian egg production as the industry continues to navi- gate this transitionary period.

Updating pullet production models to reflect differences between caged and cage-free systems is therefore imperative to the accuracy and robustness of LCA results for the Canadian egg industry.

Importantly, the results presented here indicate that conventional cages generally outperform non-organic alternatives in environmental impacts. It may seem therefore that the transition away from conven- tional cages may be a net negative for the environmental sustainability of Canadian egg production. What cannot be discounted, however, is the influence that producer experience has on determining eco-efficiency levels, in a phenomenon known as the "skipper effect" (González-García et al., 2015; Vázquez-Rowe and Tyedmers, 2013). Though the impact of producer experience on eco-efficiency levels has not be assessed in egg production systems, it is possible such a rela- tionship still exists. It is therefore likely that, as farmers continue to transition away from conventional cages and gain experience working in alternative systems, resource-use efficiency in these systems would in- crease, further decreasing environmental impacts. This possibility un- derscores the importance of continued monitoring of the environmental impacts of Canadian egg production as the industry continues to navi- gate this transitionary period.
61: Compared to the results previously presented by Pelletier (2017), the LCA models developed here demonstrate general reductions in input levels, and life cycle environmental impacts. The models developed using this updated data further build on Pelletier (2017) by introducing three key changes: the differentiation of free-run production systems into single- and multi-tier systems; the adoption of updated models for pea production used in feed production models; and the performance of quantitative uncertainty assessment. Additional environmental impacts not previously assessed are also included here.

The differentiation between single- and multi- tier free run systems in this analysis is a key development compared to Pelletier (2017). Free run production is currently the third most widespread production system in Canada, behind conventional and enriched cage production (Egg Farmers of Canada, 2020a). The proportion of farms employing free run systems is expected to grow as the industry transitions away from con- ventional cages. Importantly, the development of single- and multi-tier free run production system models as presented here will play an important role in informing egg farmers and policy makers of the environmental sustainability benefits and trade-offs associated with these systems. The results presented here, for example, indicate that

environmental performance in single-tier free run systems is the same or better than that in multi-tier systems for all impacts assessed, both of which outperformed in most cases by enriched production systems. This knowledge will aid farmers in making informed decisions as they continue to navigate the ongoing housing system transition taking place in Canada. Of note, however, this study did not take into account po- tential differences in land use between different systems. Lower acceptable stocking densities in cage-free systems necessitate greater farm-level land use by these systems, which may be exacerbated particularly in organic systems due to higher levels of land use in organic field crop production systems (Pelletier et al., 2008b). Differences in cumulative land use between housing systems should also be taken into account in sustainability decision making, as increases in organic egg production may have large-scale ripple effects in field-crop production systems around how land is allocated between conventional and organic field crops, and how crop products are allocated between human and animal consumption (Pelletier et al., 2008b). There may also be trade-offs to consider across other environmental impacts not assessed here.

Further, the use of updated models for pea production represents the first step in necessary updates to all of the feed input models presented in Pelletier (2017). Feed inputs make the greatest contribution to all of the impacts assessed, demonstrating the importance of robust, regionally specific feed crop production and feed formulation models in egg pro- duction LCAs. A recent review of Canadian agri-food LCI models indi- cated that current, Canadian-specific models for production of conventional and organic field crops, fertilizers, plant protection com- pounds, and other agricultural support activities are lacking (Turner et al., 2020). Such models must continue to be developed to decrease uncertainty associated with modelling of feed production. This would be of great benefit to all livestock industries in Canada, as feed inputs are consistently identified as key drivers of total life cycle environmental impacts across many livestock systems (Arsenault et al., 2009; Beau- chemin et al., 2011; McGeough et al., 2012; Pelletier, 2017). Improved modeling of feed production could also facilitate multi-objective opti- mization of feed formulations based on both least economic- and envi- ronmental cost, a strategy that could result in significant decreases in the environmental impacts of livestock systems (Heidari et al., 2021).

Further developments must also be made in modelling of pullet production, which makes large contributions to many of the environ- mental impacts assessed. Robust, Canadian specific data characterizing cage-free pullet production is lacking, and the caged-pullet production models used here were the same as those used in Pelletier (2017). While
Fig. 5. Relative ranking of environmental impacts per tonne of eggs produced in each housing system, across all impact categories.

pullet-related impacts have decreased compared to those presented in Pelletier (2017), the magnitude of the potential decreases may be underestimated given the absence of adequate data, particularly for cage-free pullet systems. Golden et al. (2012), for example, note that pullets reared in cage-free systems with range access consumed 0.79 kg/bird less feed than their cage-reared counterparts. The authors note that this is likely due to the replacement of feed with foraged materials, which allowed cage-free pullets to consume comparable amount of protein, despite consuming less formulated feed overall compared to caged pullets. The authors also note that there was no significant dif- ference in mortality rate between the two pullet systems (Golden et al., 2012). Similarly, differences in manure-management practices between caged and cage-free pullet production systems must also be considered, given the large contribution to environmental impacts made by manure-related emissions. Updating pullet production models to reflect differences between caged and cage-free systems is therefore imperative to the accuracy and robustness of LCA results for the Canadian egg industry.

Importantly, the results presented here indicate that conventional cages generally outperform non-organic alternatives in environmental impacts. It may seem therefore that the transition away from conven- tional cages may be a net negative for the environmental sustainability of Canadian egg production. What cannot be discounted, however, is the influence that producer experience has on determining eco-efficiency levels, in a phenomenon known as the "skipper effect" (González-García et al., 2015; Vázquez-Rowe and Tyedmers, 2013). Though the impact of producer experience on eco-efficiency levels has not be assessed in egg production systems, it is possible such a rela- tionship still exists. It is therefore likely that, as farmers continue to transition away from conventional cages and gain experience working in alternative systems, resource-use efficiency in these systems would in- crease, further decreasing environmental impacts. This possibility un- derscores the importance of continued monitoring of the environmental impacts of Canadian egg production as the industry continues to navi- gate this transitionary period.

63: As the Canadian egg industry continues to transition to alternative production systems, farmers, policy makers, and other key stakeholders will require robust information to make informed decisions about preferred housing type and potential sustainability implications. The results presented herein will provide valuable information for the Ca- nadian egg industry to progress towards sustainability goals outlined by Egg Farmers of Canada in their inaugural sustainability report (Egg Farmers of Canada, 2020b). In the future, research into the environ- mental impacts of Canadian egg production systems, and potential impact mitigation strategies must continue, particularly with respect to sustainable feed formulations and pullet production systems. Further, the environmental impacts investigated here must also be considered alongside other sustainability impacts, such as economic and animal welfare impacts, to ensure net-positive outcomes of sustainability deci- sion making in the Canadian egg industry.
